title: Scripts, indentation and main guard

canonicalExplanation.summary: A Python script is parsed with indentation defining suites and executes top-level statements in order; an if __name__ == '__main__' guard separates importable definitions from command entry behavior.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A file defines main(), keeps helpers importable, and calls main only under the conventional name guard so tests can import it without running the command. Your goal is to write and run a saved script while explaining its top-level execution. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: pf computing execution; pf tools terminal editor; Interactive interpreter. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Language-specific operational model
sections.body: A Python script is parsed with indentation defining suites and executes top-level statements in order; an if __name__ == '__main__' guard separates importable definitions from command entry behavior. This model is applied through the declared Python: Beginner to Intermediate behavior rather than a language-neutral slogan.

sections.heading: Boundary and failure model
sections.body: The guard does not create a function scope, importing executes unguarded top-level code once per module object, whitespace mixing can fail, and direct versus module launch changes package context. The learner must reject this documented misconception: Python ignores indentation once code is syntactically valid and importing a script never executes its top-level statements.

includes: Create executable modules with meaningful layout, indentation and optional entry-point guard.
Write and run a saved script while explaining its top-level execution.
excludes: The guard does not create a function scope, importing executes unguarded top-level code once per module object, whitespace mixing can fail, and direct versus module launch changes package context.

outcomes: Write and run a saved script while explaining its top-level execution.

examples.title: Scripts, indentation and main guard: direct language case
examples.situation: A file defines main(), keeps helpers importable, and calls main only under the conventional name guard so tests can import it without running the command.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Observe the concrete Python: Beginner to Intermediate state or tool result: A file defines main(), keeps helpers importable, and calls main only under the conventional name guard so tests can import it without running the command.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.

Step 3 — Verify the result with this correction: Indentation is part of Python block syntax, and import executes top-level code; keep reusable definitions at module level and put command effects behind an explicit main guard.
examples.result: The example meets the atomic outcome because Indentation is part of Python block syntax, and import executes top-level code; keep reusable definitions at module level and put command effects behind an explicit main guard.

examples.title: Scripts, indentation and main guard: boundary and transfer case
examples.situation: A module with an unguarded network call performs that call during import, so the side effect is moved behind an explicit function and entry guard.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Name what changed from the direct case: A module with an unguarded network call performs that call during import, so the side effect is moved behind an explicit function and entry guard.

Step 2 — Keep the language boundary explicit: The guard does not create a function scope, importing executes unguarded top-level code once per module object, whitespace mixing can fail, and direct versus module launch changes package context.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting but incorrect rule: Python ignores indentation once code is syntactically valid and importing a script never executes its top-level statements.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Write and run a saved script while explaining its top-level execution.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: Python ignores indentation once code is syntactically valid and importing a script never executes its top-level statements.
misconceptions.correction: Indentation is part of Python block syntax, and import executes top-level code; keep reusable definitions at module level and put command effects behind an explicit main guard.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Predict both examples and identify the first observable contradiction in this belief: Python ignores indentation once code is syntactically valid and importing a script never executes its top-level statements.

analogy.example: an experiment notebook that becomes a saved procedure executed by one identified interpreter in a known workspace can introduce Scripts, indentation and main guard, after the canonical Python: Beginner to Intermediate rule is stated first.
usefulFor: REPL experiments, scripts, environments, and tracebacks
limitations: import caching, module paths, display hooks, indentation grammar, and traceback chaining are Python-specific
